title: Programmable Superradiance in an Interacting Qubit Array
authors: Botao Du, Qihao Guo, Ruichao Ma
abstract: When multiple quantum emitters couple to a common electromagnetic environment, interference in their collective radiative dynamics gives rise to superradiance and subradiance. In regimes where coherent interactions and collective dissipation compete, the microscopic many-body dynamics and quantum correlations among the emitters that underlie superradiance and subradiance are theoretically challenging and remain experimentally elusive, even though collective emission has been observed in many physical systems. Here, we realize a superconducting qubit array coupled to a common microwave waveguide that mediates collective dissipation, with simultaneous access to coherent interactions and microscopic measurements of many-body dynamics. Engineered qubit-waveguide couplings with tunable amplitude and phase enable control of collective interference and the resulting super- and subradiant states. Leveraging site-resolved control and readout, we directly observe the microscopic decay dynamics of multi-qubit states across different excitation manifolds and track the evolution of populations and tunable quantum correlations. We reveal collective decay in regimes beyond the ideal Dicke model, where strong qubit-qubit interactions stabilize superradiance and subradiance against local dephasing and reshape decay pathways through spatially and spectrally structured many-body eigenstates. Our results establish a flexible platform for exploring collective phenomena in many-body quantum optics and driven-dissipative approaches to robust quantum information processing.
summary: This paper demonstrates a programmable platform for observing superradiance and subradiance in an interacting superconducting qubit array. By controlling the phase of engineered waveguide coupling, the researchers can manipulate collective interference and the spatial structure of many-body correlations. This work establishes a versatile tool for exploring driven-dissipative dynamics in many-body quantum optics.
main_problem: Understanding and controlling the microscopic many-body dynamics and quantum correlations underlying superradiance and subradiance in interacting qubit arrays, specifically moving beyond the idealized Dicke model.
main_result: The researchers demonstrated programmable control over collective decay by tuning the phase of engineered waveguide coupling, revealing that strong qubit-qubit interactions can stabilize super- and subradiant states and reshape decay pathways.
method: The team used parametric flux modulation to engineer Purcell decay into a 1D microwave waveguide and employed site-resolved readout to track population decay and many-body correlations.
model_or_system: A superconducting transmon qubit array coupled to a common 1D microwave waveguide via resonators, where the dynamics are described by a non-Hermitian effective Hamiltonian including nearest-neighbor exchange interactions and engineered collective dissipation.
key_observables: Multi-qubit state populations, emission rate, instantaneous decay rate, number fluctuations, number-squeezing parameter, and transverse/longitudinal spin correlations.
important_parameters: Engineered decay rate (gamma_wg ~ 15 kHz), coherent interaction strength (J), relative coupling phase (theta = 0 or pi), and the regime where J >> gamma_wg.
main_assumptions: The system is treated in the dispersive limit; higher transmon levels are accounted for via perturbative virtual transitions; the resonator degrees of freedom are adiabatically eliminated.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the device layout and waveguide QED model; Figure 2 demonstrates programmable decay and phase control in two qubits; Figure 4 shows the decay dynamics and correlation patterns in a four-qubit array; Figure 5 illustrates the energy spectrum and many-body energy trajectories.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of collective decay in interacting systems, describes the superconducting circuit platform and engineering of dissipation, presents results for two-qubit phase control, extends the study to many-body dynamics and correlations in a four-qubit array, and concludes with a discussion on the implications for driven-dissipative quantum information processing.
why_interesting: It provides a rare experimental realization of programmable many-body dissipation, allowing for the direct observation of how coherent interactions compete with collective decay to reshape quantum correlations and energy landscapes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Waveguide-coupled qubit array and engineered dissipation. (a) Device layout of the superconducting cir- cuit, comprising an array of four frequency-tunable transmon qubits (red) with local flux bias (pink), each coupled to a res- onator (blue), which in turn couples to a common waveguide (green). (b) Effective waveguide QED model of the device. (c) Energy-level diagram. The qubits (red) are initially far detuned from the resonators (blue); parametric flux modu- lation generates qubit sidebands (orange) near the resonator frequencies, enabling enhanced Purcell decay through the res- onator linewidth into the broadband waveguide, as illustrated by the spectral density S(ω). FIG. 2. Programmable collective decay in two interacting qubits. (a) Schematic of two resonant qubits Q1 and Q2 with coherent coupling J, forming symmetric and antisymmetric single-excitation eigenstates |±⟩. Engineered waveguide cou- pling with equal amplitude γwg and relative phase θ = ϕ1−ϕ2 enables interference of the emitted fields. (b) Measured popu- lation decay of |±⟩for (i) θ = 0 and (ii) θ = π, showing rever- sal of superradiant and subradiant behavior. (c) Extracted engineered decay rates Γ± wg as a function of θ, after subtract- ing intrinsic qubit decay, demonstrating continuous control of collective emission. Fig. 2b(i) shows the corresponding exponential popula- tion decay of the two states, demonstrating the contrast between superradiant and subradiant dynamics. We de- fine the total excitation number as N = P

FIG. 3. Collective decay of single-excitation eigenstates. Measured waveguide decay rates Γwg for single-excitation eigenstates of qubit arrays with size up to Nq = 4 under uniform engineered dissipation (θ = 0, with single-qubit rate γwg = 2π × 15 kHz, as plotted for Nq = 1). Intrinsic de- cay rates have been subtracted. Illustrations adjacent to each data point indicate the corresponding eigenstate wavefunc- tion amplitudes. The highest-energy mode (m = 1) exhibits superradiant decay with a rate that scales approximately lin- early with Nq (dashed line).

FIG. 4. Dynamics of superradiance in a four-qubit array. For symmetric coupling into the waveguide (a), the superradiant manifold consists of the highest-energy eigenstates at different excitation numbers, as indicated in the many-body energy spectrum shown in the rotating frame of the qubits. These states approximate the symmetric Dicke states, while all other eigenstates are subradiant.
